story:
The city trembled like prey, its neon a nervous pulse. I slid through its seams, not the plague but its whisper, obsessed with one heartbeat—Nova. I could not hurt her; the thought curdled me. But the will that commanded me—the Composer, the Master—would carve her friends to force her hand. So I lingered where I should have killed.

Nova watched the diner TV blare about a “Shadow Beast” while Evan joked and Maya and Leo worried over her sleepless eyes. The lie on Nova’s tongue—just insomnia—tasted like rust. Night after night the dreams came: alleys cold as breath, my voice against her skin, fear tangled with a pull she hated.

Glass blew inward. I rose from darkness, towering, voice like a cold bell: join me or your friends suffer. Nova flared with power; her psychic shove skimmed my ribs. Leo choked, proof of consequence. Then the streetlight burst, a white blade through my vision. While I clawed at the pain, Nova ripped Leo from my hold and dragged them through smoke and corridors. They called it faulty wiring; Nova knew it was her, and I knew it too.

She fled to the old library’s dust and hush, digging for answers. My voice slid between the stacks: the fire you fear feeds me; you belong to me. She ran. I tore through shelves, pinned her, claws thudding into wood. I told her the truth I despised: I loved her; I was a marionette; my mercy had limits set by another hand. Test me again and her friends would pay. Lamps shattered under her will. I roared and she vanished, my vow shaking the rafters: you can’t run forever.

By morning the hospital was a corridor of bright cold. Leo sedated and breathing through plastic. Maya trembling under shadow-inked scratches. Evan pale, arm splinted. Nova slid down tile, grief flooding in. This was on her. She went home not to hide, but to wait. When I arrived, storm-eyed and furious, she met rage with gallows humor until I slammed her to the wall, then dropped, crushed by my own begging. I told her everything: the Composer wanted her gift tuned to obedience; my threats were a script, my love the only unscripted thing left. She offered the clean end—kill me—but I told her death wouldn’t stop a hunger that could find new puppets. I named the truth: her friends breathed because I allowed it. She broke, then hardened. What do you want me to do? Come with me, I said. Embrace it. Become the weapon I couldn’t.

Our kiss sealed a terrible pact. The world fell away into a cavern like a wound. The Master hung there, a formless void that spoke in erasure. It pressed visions of not-death but deletion. Nova understood: the key wasn’t dying; it was choosing. She said yes—not to him, but to herself. Bones re-sang, skin scaled, a dragon unfurled. The void recoiled. She cut the strings—his from the city, his from me. Silence collapsed on itself. I coughed up smoke and finally saw through my own eyes. It’s over, she said. We were free, altered.

Weeks later, Oakhaven traded terror for rumors: rain where there should be fire, robbers stuck by fused tires, two figures—one of light, one of shadow—moving like a promise. Nova flinched at the dragon in her mirror, hating the monster and loving the reason for it. My arms around her were proof: the darkness hadn’t been destroyed; it had been redeemed. Together we weren’t nightmares. We were shelter. The necessary night that makes the dawn worth fighting for.
